title: Classification of Cardiomyopathy
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium
authors: Lynne Warner Stevenson, Neal K. Lakdawala, Joseph Loscalzo

items.title: Understanding the Phenotypic Overlap in Cardiomyopathies
items.content: Cardiomyopathies traditionally classified into hypertrophic, dilated, and restrictive types can exhibit overlapping features over time. This overlap is due to therapeutic interventions that may reverse remodeling in dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) or the evolution of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) into a reduced ejection fraction phenotype.
keyPoints: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is characterized by increased left ventricular wall thickness ($ \geq $13–15 mm) and normal/high ejection fraction.
Dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) typically presents with a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) $ \leq $0.50, often $ \leq $0.40 in clinical presentations.
Restrictive cardiomyopathy (RCM) is defined by abnormal diastolic physiology rather than morphology alone.
DCMs can undergo 'reverse remodeling' with therapy, increasing ejection fraction and reducing ventricular size.
Approximately 5% of HCM patients may evolve to a reduced ejection fraction phenotype (HCM-rEF).
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · Classification of Cardiomyopathy

items.title: Genetic and Acquired Influences on Cardiomyopathies
items.content: The classification of cardiomyopathies is evolving with the recognition of genetic variants and immune response pathways that contribute to both heritable and acquired forms. This includes arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) and conditions like sarcoidosis and Chagas’ disease, which can mimic inherited cardiomyopathies.
keyPoints: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) is characterized by life-threatening arrhythmias and abnormal right ventricular structure/function.
New imaging techniques may identify genetic variants causing heritable cardiomyopathies before clinical disease manifests.
Inherited immune response pathways can contribute to myocarditis, leading to cardiomyopathy.
Sarcoidosis and Chagas’ disease can cause phenotypes with cardiomyopathy and ventricular arrhythmias, qualifying as ACM-RV or ACM-LV.
Cardiomyopathies may follow a two-hit model where genetic predisposition is triggered by acquired conditions like infections or toxic exposures.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · Classification of Cardiomyopathy

items.subtopic: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Recognizing Cardiomyopathy Through Symptoms and Initial Evaluation
items.content: Cardiomyopathy can present with exertional intolerance, breathlessness, fatigue, arrhythmias, or embolic events. Congestive symptoms like fluid retention and elevated heart filling pressures may manifest as shortness of breath, orthopnea, edema, and abdominal symptoms. The term 'congestive heart failure' is a nonspecific syndrome requiring thorough evaluation to identify the underlying etiology.
keyPoints: Early symptoms include exertional intolerance with breathlessness or fatigue.
Arrhythmias and embolic events can be presenting features of unrecognized cardiomyopathy.
'Congestive heart failure' describes a nonspecific syndrome, not a diagnosis or etiology.
Initial evaluation involves detailed clinical history and examination to identify cardiac, genetic, and systemic causes.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Imaging Modalities in the Evaluation of Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Echocardiography is the primary imaging modality for assessing cardiomyopathy, providing insights into morphology and function. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) complements this by offering detailed myocardial tissue characterization, identifying patterns of fibrosis through late gadolinium enhancement, and detecting focal and diffuse inflammation via T1 and T2 mapping.
keyPoints: Echocardiography is the initial imaging modality for defining morphology and function in cardiomyopathy.
MRI provides additional information on myocardial tissue characterization.
Late gadolinium enhancement in MRI indicates patterns of fibrosis.
T1 and T2 mapping in MRI detect focal and diffuse inflammation.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.question: Which phenotype of cardiomyopathy is characterized by increased left ventricular wall thickness and normal or high ejection fraction?
items.explanation: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is characterized by increased left ventricular wall thickness and normal or high ejection fraction.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · Classification of Cardiomyopathy

items.question: What defines dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) in terms of left ventricular ejection fraction?
items.explanation: Dilated cardiomyopathy is defined when the left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) is ≤0.50, but most clinical presentations are with LVEF ≤0.40.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · Classification of Cardiomyopathy

items.question: Which condition can evolve in approximately 5% of patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy to a reduced ejection fraction?
options: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction (HCM-rEF)
items.explanation: Approximately 5% of patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy evolve to a phenotype known as HCM with reduced ejection fraction (HCM-rEF).
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · Classification of Cardiomyopathy

items.subtopic: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: Which symptom is commonly an early presentation of cardiomyopathy?
options: Exertional intolerance with breathlessness or fatigue
items.explanation: Early symptoms often reflect exertional intolerance with breathlessness or fatigue.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: What is a common presenting event of unrecognized cardiomyopathy?
items.explanation: Arrhythmias are often presenting events of unrecognized cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: What imaging modality is initially used to define the morphology and function in suspected cardiomyopathy?
items.explanation: Echocardiography remains the initial imaging modality to define morphology and function.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.statement: Restrictive cardiomyopathies are primarily defined by their morphological characteristics.
items.explanation: Restrictive cardiomyopathies are often defined less by morphology than by evidence of abnormal diastolic physiology.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · Classification of Cardiomyopathy

items.statement: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) is characterized by life-threatening arrhythmias and abnormal right ventricular structure.
items.explanation: ARVC is indeed characterized by life-threatening arrhythmias and abnormal right ventricular structure.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · Classification of Cardiomyopathy

items.subtopic: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.statement: Congestive heart failure is considered a specific diagnosis of cardiomyopathy.
items.explanation: 'Congestive heart failure' should not be considered a diagnosis or an etiology of cardiomyopathy but a nonspecific syndrome.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.statement: Magnetic resonance imaging can provide information on myocardial tissue characterization in cardiomyopathy.
items.explanation: MRI is increasingly used to provide further information on myocardial tissue characterization.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.assertion: The classification of cardiomyopathies is evolving with new phenotype-genotype connections.
items.reason: New imaging techniques can identify pathogenic genetic variants that lead to heritable cardiomyopathies prior to clinical disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain why the classification is evolving.
items.reference: Classification of Cardiomyopathy · Classification of Cardiomyopathy

items.subtopic: CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND EVALUATION OF CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.assertion: Initial evaluation of possible cardiomyopathy includes a detailed clinical history and examination.
items.reason: This helps to guide subsequent evaluation by seeking clues to cardiac, genetic, and systemic causes of heart disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
